## **Near-Optimal No-Regret Learning in General Games**

**Constantinos Daskalakis** MIT CSAIL `costis@csail.mit.edu`

**Maxwell Fishelson Noah Golowich** MIT CSAIL MIT CSAIL `maxfish@mit.edu nzg@mit.edu`

## **Abstract**

We show that Optimistic Hedge – a common variant of multiplicative-weightsupdates with recency bias – attains poly(log _T_ ) regret in multi-player general-sum games. In particular, when every player of the game uses Optimistic Hedge to iteratively update her strategy in response to the history of play so far, then after _T_ rounds of interaction, _each player_ experiences total regret that is poly(log _T_ ). Our bound improves, exponentially, the _O_ ( _T_[1] _[/]_[2] ) regret attainable by standard no-regret learners in games, the _O_ ( _T_[1] _[/]_[4] ) regret attainable by no-regret learners with recency bias [SALS15], and the _O_ ( _T_[1] _[/]_[6] ) bound that was recently shown for Optimistic Hedge in the special case of two-player games [CP20]. A corollary of our bound is that Optimistic Hedge converges to coarse correlated equilibrium in general games at a rate of _O_[˜] 1 . � _T_ �

## **1 Introduction**

Online learning has a long history that is intimately related to the development of game theory, convex optimization, and machine learning. One of its earliest instantiations can be traced to Brown’s proposal [Bro49] of fictitious play as a method to solve two-player zero-sum games. Indeed, as shown by [Rob51], when the players of (zero-sum) matrix game use fictitious play to iteratively update their actions in response to each other’s history of play, the resulting dynamics converge in the following sense: the product of the empirical distributions of strategies for each player converges to the set of Nash equilibria in the game, though the rate of convergence is now known to be exponentially slow [DP14]. Moreover, such convergence to Nash equilibria fails in non-zero-sum games [Sha64].

The slow convergence of play to Nash equilibria in zero-sum matrix games and nonconvergence in general-sum games can be mitigated by appealing to the pioneering works [Bla54, Han57] and the ensuing literature on no-regret learning [CBL06]. It is known that if both players of a zero-sum matrix game experience regret that is at most _"_ ( _T_ ), the product of the players’ empirical distributions of strategies is an _O_ ( _"_ ( _T_ ) _/T_ )-approximate Nash equilibrium. More generally, if each player of a general-sum, multi-player game experiences regret that is at most _"_ ( _T_ ), the empirical distribution of joint strategies converges to a coarse correlated equilibrium[1] of the game, at a rate of _O_ ( _"_ ( _T_ ) _/T_ ). Importantly, a multitude of online learning algorithms, such as the celebrated Hedge and Follow-The-Perturbed-Leader algorithms, guarantee adversarial regret _O_ ( _pT_ ) [CBL06]. Thus, when such algorithms are employed by all players in a game, their _O_ ( _pT_ ) regret implies convergence to coarse correlated equilibria (and Nash equilibria of matrix games) at a rate of _O_ (1 _/pT_ ).

While standard no-regret learners guarantee _O_ ( _pT_ ) regret for each player in a game, the players can do better by employing specialized no-regret learning procedures. Indeed, it was established by [DDK11] that there exists a somewhat complex no-regret learner based on Nesterov’s excessive gap technique [Nes05], which guarantees _O_ (log _T_ ) regret to each player of a two-player zero-sum game.

This represents an exponential improvement over the regret guaranteed by standard no-regret learners. More generally, [SALS15] established that if players of a multi-player, general-sum game use any algorithm from the family of Optimistic Mirror Descent (MD) or Optimistic Follow-the-RegularizedLeader (FTRL) algorithms (which are analogoues of the MD and FTRL algorithms, respectively, with recency bias), each player enjoys regret that is _O_ ( _T_[1] _[/]_[4] ). This was recently improved by [CP20] to _O_ ( _T_[1] _[/]_[6] ) in the special case of two-player games in which the players use Optimistic Hedge, a particularly simple representative from both the Optimistic MD and Optimistic FTRL families.

The above results for general-sum games represent significant improvements over the _O_ ( _pT_ ) regret attainable by standard no-regret learners, but are not as dramatic as the logarithmic regret that has been shown attainable by no-regret learners, albeit more complex ones, in 2-player zero-sum games (e.g., [DDK11]). Indeed, despite extensive work on no-regret learning, understanding the optimal regret that can be guaranteed by no-regret learning algorithms in general-sum games has remained elusive. This question is especially intruiging in light of experiments suggesting that polylogarithmic regret should be attainable [SALS15, HAM21]. In this paper we settle this question by showing that no-regret learners can guarantee polylogarithmic regret to each player in general-sum multi-player games. Moreover, this regret is attainable by a particularly simple algorithm – Optimistic Hedge:

**Theorem 1.1** (Abbreviated version of Theorem 3.1) **.** _Suppose that m players play a general-sum multi-player game, with a finite set of n strategies per player, over T rounds. Suppose also that each player uses Optimistic Hedge to update her strategy in every round, as a function of the history of play so far. Then each player experiences O_ ( _m ·_ log _n ·_ log[4] _T_ ) _regret._

An immediate corollary of Theorem 1.1 is that the empirical distribution of play is a _O m_ log _nT_ log[4] _T_ - ⇣ ⌘ approximate coarse correlated equilibrium (CCE) of the game. We remark that Theorem 1.1 bounds the total regret experienced by _each player_ of the multi-player game, which is the most standard regret objective for no-regret learning in games, and which is essential to achieve convergence to CCE. For the looser objective of the _average_ of all players’ regrets, [RS13b] established a _O_ (log _n_ ) bound for Optimistic Hedge in two-player zero-sum games, and [SALS15] generalized this bound, to _O_ ( _m_ log _n_ ) in _m_ -player general-sum games. Note that since some players may experience _negative regret_ [HAM21], the average of the players’ regrets cannot be used in general to bound the maximum regret experienced by any individual player. Finally, we remark that several results in the literature posit no-regret learning as a model of agents’ rational behavior; for instance, [Rou09, ST13, RST17] show that no-regret learners in smooth games enjoy strong Price-of-Anarchy bounds. By showing that _each agent_ can obtain very small regret in games by playing Optimistic Hedge, Theorem 1.1

2

strengthens the plausability of the common assumption made in this literature that each agent will choose to use such a no-regret algorithm.

## **1.1 Related work**

Table 1 summarizes the prior works that aim to establish optimal regret bounds for no-regret learners in games. We remark that [CP20] shows that the regret of Hedge is ⌦( _pT_ ) even in 2-player games where each player has 2 actions, meaning that optimism is necessary to obtain fast rates. The table also includes a recent result of [HAM21] showing that when the players in a 2-player zero-sum game with _n_ actions per player use a variant of Optimistic Hedge with adaptive step size (a special case of their algorithms DS-OptMD and OptDA), each player has log _[O]_[(1)] _n_ regret. The techniques of [HAM21] differ substantially from ours: the result in [HAM21] is based on showing that the joint strategies _x_[(] _[t]_[)] rapidly converge, pointwise, to a Nash equilibrium _x[?]_ . Such a result seems very unlikely to extend to our setting of general-sum games, since finding an approximate Nash equilibrium even in 2-player games is PPAD-complete [CDT09]. We also remark that the earlier work [KHSC18] shows that each player’s regret is at most _O_ (log _T ·_ log _n_ ) when they use a certain algorithm based on Optimistic MD in 2-player zero-sum games; their technique is heavily tailored to 2-player zero-sum games, relying on the notion of duality in such a setting.

[FLL[+] 16] shows that one can obtain fast rates in games for a broader class of algorithms (e.g., including Hedge) if one adopts a relaxed (approximate) notion of optimality. [WL18] uses optimism to obtain adaptive regret bounds for bandit problems. Many recent papers (e.g., [DP19, GPD20, LGNPw21, HAM21, WLZL21, AIMM21]) have studied the _last-iterate_ convergence of algorithms from the Optimistic Mirror Descent family, which includes Optimistic Hedge. Finally, a long line of papers (e.g., [HMcW[+] 03, DFP[+] 10, KLP11, BCM12, PP16, BP18, MPP18, BP19, CP19, VGFL[+] 20]) has studied the dynamics of learning algorithms in games. Essentially all of these papers do not use optimism, and many of them show _non-convergence_ (e.g., divergence or recurrence) of the iterates of various learning algorithms such as FTRL and Mirror Descent when used in games.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a fundamental and long-standing open question in online learning and algorithmic game theory: the optimal regret attainable by no-regret learners in multi-player general-sum games. The central result—that Optimistic Hedge achieves poly(log T) regret per player—represents an exponential improvement over the prior O(T^{1/4}) bound of [SALS15] and the recent O(T^{1/6}) bound of [CP20] for the two-player case, and it essentially closes the gap with known results for two-player zero-sum games. The contribution is significant, timely, and well-motivated, and the introduction situates the work clearly within a rich body of prior literature, with a helpful comparison table summarizing the landscape. The writing is generally clear and the claims are precisely stated, though the presentation could be tightened: the abstract and introduction are somewhat repetitive, several minor typographical issues appear (e.g., "intruiging," inconsistent math formatting in the extracted table, and awkward rendering of exponents), and readers less familiar with Optimistic Hedge would benefit from an earlier, more intuitive explanation of *why* recency bias enables such dramatic improvements—ideally a proof-sketch paragraph highlighting the key technical innovation that allows the authors to break past the T^{1/6} barrier. The most significant substantive concern, which cannot be assessed from the introduction alone, is whether the polylogarithmic bound has favorable dependence on the number of players m and actions n (the table suggests linear in m and log n, which would be excellent), and whether a matching lower bound or discussion of optimality is provided—since the title claims "near-optimal," this deserves careful justification in the body of the paper.

Written as a modification of Hedge's one-step exponent, this is equivalent to using 2ℓ^(t) − ℓ^(t−1) as predictor only under a specific convention; more commonly the predictor is stated simply as ℓ^(t) itself, with the 'doubling' coming from the FTRL formulation. The formula 'ℓ^(t) + (ℓ^(t) − ℓ^(t−1))' for the predictor of the following iteration's loss is a linear extrapolation, which is one valid choice, but the text frames it as THE definition without making clear this is a specific modeling choice. progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper appears to make a strong and potentially important contribution: establishing polylogarithmic-in-\(T\) regret for Optimistic Hedge in multi-player general-sum games would substantially advance the literature on fast learning in games and significantly improve over the previously known \(T^{1/4}\) and \(T^{1/6}\) rates. The introduction situates the result well relative to classical no-regret learning, equilibrium convergence, and recent optimistic-method analyses, and the high-level motivation is compelling. However, the presentation in the excerpt has several clarity and polish issues that should be addressed. Some notation is garbled or difficult to parse—for example the abstract’s convergence-rate statement, square-root notation, and the table formatting—making key quantitative claims harder to verify. The paper should also state early and explicitly the precise assumptions under which the result holds: payoff normalization, feedback model, whether all players must use the same fixed/adaptive learning rate, dependence on the number of players \(m\) and actions \(n\), and whether the bound is uniform over time or horizon-dependent. The term “near-optimal” also needs immediate justification, ideally by citing or proving a matching lower bound up to logarithmic factors; otherwise it risks sounding overstated. Finally, while the claimed improvement is impressive, the introduction would benefit from a more transparent explanation of the main technical idea behind why Optimistic Hedge achieves polylogarithmic regret in general games, since this is the central surprising point relative to prior work. Overall, the paper seems technically significant and well motivated, but the opening would be strengthened by cleaner notation, a more readable comparison table, sharper statement of assumptions and parameter dependencies, and clearer justification of the optimality claim.

comments.title: Abstract suppresses dependence on players/actions in regret and CCE rates
comments.quote: when every player of the game uses Optimistic Hedge to iteratively update her strategy in response to the history of play so far, then after T rounds of interaction, each player experiences total regret that is poly(log T). ... A corollary of our bound is that Optimistic Hedge converges to coarse correlated equilibrium in general games at a rate of O[~](1/T).
comments.explanation: The intended high-level message is that the dependence on the time horizon is polylogarithmic, and the later formal theorem makes the full bound explicit as O(m · log n · log^4 T). For fixed finite games, the abstract’s phrasing is therefore understandable. Still, the abstract says “general games” and states “poly(log T)” regret and an O[~](1/T) CCE rate without mentioning the linear dependence on the number of players and logarithmic dependence on the number of actions. Since the paper elsewhere tracks these parameters, the abstract could mislead readers about scalability unless it clarifies that the shorthand suppresses game-parameter dependence as well as logarithms in T.

comments.title: The abbreviated theorem omits the Optimistic Hedge step-size condition
comments.quote: Theorem 1.1 (Abbreviated version of Theorem 3.1). Suppose that m players play a general-sum multi-player game, with a finite set of n strategies per player, over T rounds. Suppose also that each player uses Optimistic Hedge to update her strategy in every round, as a function of the history of play so far. Then each player experiences O(m · log n · log^4 T) regret.
comments.explanation: The intended statement is clear from the surrounding context and from the later formal Theorem 3.1: Optimistic Hedge must be run with an appropriate learning rate/step size, depending at least on the horizon and the number of players. As written, however, the abbreviated theorem sounds like the polylogarithmic regret bound holds for Optimistic Hedge with no parameter qualification. Multiplicative-weights-style algorithms are sensitive to the learning rate, and an arbitrary badly chosen step size need not satisfy the stated bound. Since this is stated as a theorem rather than only informal prose, it should say “with an appropriate step size” or refer explicitly to the step size specified in Theorem 3.1.

comments.title: “Settle” may overstate what is shown about optimal regret
comments.quote: Indeed, despite extensive work on no-regret learning, understanding the optimal regret that can be guaranteed by no-regret learning algorithms in general-sum games has remained elusive. This question is especially intruiging in light of experiments suggesting that polylogarithmic regret should be attainable [SALS15, HAM21]. In this paper we settle this question by showing that no-regret learners can guarantee polylogarithmic regret to each player in general-sum multi-player games.
comments.explanation: The paper appears to answer the important existence question of whether polylogarithmic regret is attainable in general-sum multi-player games. However, the preceding sentence frames the open problem as understanding the “optimal regret,” and the next sentence says the paper “settle[s] this question.” A polylogarithmic upper bound alone does not identify the optimal dependence on T, m, n, or the exact logarithmic powers unless accompanied by matching lower bounds. If the intended claim is only that polylogarithmic regret is attainable, the wording should avoid implying that the exact optimal regret rate is fully characterized.

comments.title: Lower bound for Hedge does not by itself show optimism is necessary
comments.quote: We remark that [CP20] shows that the regret of Hedge is Ω(√T) even in 2-player games where each player has 2 actions, meaning that optimism is necessary to obtain fast rates.
comments.explanation: The cited lower bound supports the motivation for studying Optimistic Hedge because it rules out standard Hedge as the algorithm achieving the desired fast rates. But the stated inference is stronger than what the lower bound establishes. A lower bound for Hedge shows that Hedge is insufficient; it does not prove that every non-optimistic algorithm fails, nor that optimism is necessary as a property of algorithms in general. The claim would be more accurate if phrased as “standard Hedge is insufficient” or “motivating the use of optimism.”

comments.title: Boundary and off-by-one issues in the downward finite-difference induction
comments.quote: We next show that for all 0 ≤ h ≤ H, ... This shown via downwards induction on h, namely first establishing the base case h = H by using the result of item 1 for h = H
comments.explanation: The intended argument is to compare successive finite-difference energies and then propagate a high-order bound downward. As written, however, the indexing is not fully well-defined. By Definition 4.1, an order-h finite difference of a length-T sequence is only defined up to a shortened endpoint, so sums from t = 1 to T involving D_{h+1}ℓ_i and D_hℓ_i are not literally defined near the end of the horizon unless the authors are implicitly padding, truncating, or extending the sequence. In addition, the stated base case at h = H involves the left-hand side with D_{H+1}ℓ_i, while the preceding high-order finite-difference bound is described only for orders h ≤ H. The appendix may resolve this with a boundary convention or by shifting the induction range, but the overview does not make the convention clear.

comments.title: The claimed exponential decay in finite-difference order is not immediate from the displayed bound
comments.quote: First, in Lemma 4.4 below, we find an upper bound on (D_h ℓ_i)_1 for all t ∈ [T], h ≥ 0, which decays exponentially in h for h ≤ H.
comments.explanation: The abbreviated Lemma 4.4 states a bound of the form O(mη)^h · h^{O(h)}. Such a bound decays exponentially in h only if the step size is small enough to dominate the hidden h^{O(h)} factor, e.g. if mη h^C is uniformly below a constant less than 1 for all h ≤ H. The detailed appendix may establish this through the chosen inverse-polylogarithmic step size, but the proof overview does not state the needed condition explicitly. Thus the phrase “decays exponentially in h” is stronger than what the displayed asymptotic form transparently implies on its own.

comments.title: Coordinatewise chain-rule bound does not by itself justify the needed ℓ1 bound on strategy differences
comments.quote: To complete the inductive step it remains to upper bound the quantities ||(D_h x_i)^{(t)}||_1 for i ∈ [m], t ∈ [T − h]. ... Recalling that our goal is to bound |(D_h x_i(j))^{(t+1)}| for each t, we can do so by using Lemma 4.5 with φ = φ_{t,i,j} and λ = O(mη).
comments.explanation: The intended reduction is that losses depend linearly on other players’ mixed strategies, so controlling finite differences of the strategies controls finite differences of losses. However, the overview moves from bounding each coordinate |(D_h x_i(j))^{(t)}| via a scalar softmax map to the desired bound on ||(D_h x_i)^{(t)}||_1. A coordinatewise bound would generally introduce an n_i factor when summed over actions, which would be incompatible with the claimed logarithmic dependence on the number of actions. There may be an additional argument using softmax normalization or total-variation control of the whole distribution, but it is not stated in the overview. As written, the passage from scalar coordinate bounds to the required dimension-sensitive ℓ1 estimate is not justified.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper appears to make a strong and potentially important contribution: establishing polylogarithmic-in-\(T\) regret for Optimistic Hedge in multi-player general-sum games would substantially advance the literature on fast learning in games and significantly improve over the previously known \(T^{1/4}\) and \(T^{1/6}\) rates. The introduction situates the result well relative to classical no-regret learning, equilibrium convergence, and recent optimistic-method analyses, and the high-level motivation is compelling. However, the presentation in the excerpt has several clarity and polish issues that should be addressed. Some notation is garbled or difficult to parse—for example the abstract’s convergence-rate statement, square-root notation, and the table formatting—making key quantitative claims harder to verify. The paper should also state early and explicitly the precise assumptions under which the result holds: payoff normalization, feedback model, whether all players must use the same fixed/adaptive learning rate, dependence on the number of players \(m\) and actions \(n\), and whether the bound is uniform over time or horizon-dependent. The term “near-optimal” also needs immediate justification, ideally by citing or proving a matching lower bound up to logarithmic factors; otherwise it risks sounding overstated. Finally, while the claimed improvement is impressive, the introduction would benefit from a more transparent explanation of the main technical idea behind why Optimistic Hedge achieves polylogarithmic regret in general games, since this is the central surprising point relative to prior work. Overall, the paper seems technically significant and well motivated, but the opening would be strengthened by cleaner notation, a more readable comparison table, sharper statement of assumptions and parameter dependencies, and clearer justification of the optimality claim.

comments.title: Abstract suppresses non-logarithmic dependence on the number of players
comments.quote: A corollary of our bound is that Optimistic Hedge converges to coarse correlated equilibrium in general games at a rate of _O_[˜] 1 .  _T_
comments.explanation: I initially read this as the standard no-regret-to-CCE implication: if each player has regret polylogarithmic in T, the empirical distribution is an approximate CCE with error regret/T. Looking at the formal theorem in the provided context, however, the regret bound is not only poly(log T): it is O(m · log n · log^4 T), so the corresponding CCE error is O(m log n log^4 T / T). If m and n are treated as fixed game parameters, the abstract’s \tilde O(1/T) shorthand is reasonable, and the later theorem/table do make the dependence explicit. What remains mildly problematic is that the abstract says “general games” and the table caption defines \tilde O as hiding factors polynomial in log T, not linear dependence on m. A reader tracking scalability in the number of players could therefore incorrectly infer a rate independent of m.

comments.title: “poly(log T) regret” omits action-set and player-count dependence
comments.quote: when every player of the game uses Optimistic Hedge to iteratively update her strategy in response to the history of play so far, then after _T_ rounds of interaction, _each player_ experiences total regret that is poly(log _T_ ).
comments.explanation: The intended high-level message is clear: the dependence on the time horizon improves from a polynomial in T to a polynomial in log T. The later formal statement resolves the exact bound as O(m · log n · log^4 T), so for a fixed finite game the sentence is accurate as a statement about T-dependence. Still, taken literally, “total regret that is poly(log T)” suppresses a linear dependence on the number of players and a logarithmic dependence on the number of actions. This is not a mathematical error in the main theorem, but it is a mild ambiguity in the abstract because the result is for general multi-player games where m and n are meaningful parameters.

comments.title: The abbreviated theorem omits the Optimistic Hedge step-size condition
comments.quote: **Theorem 1.1** (Abbreviated version of Theorem 3.1) **.** _Suppose that m players play a general-sum multi-player game, with a finite set of n strategies per player, over T rounds. Suppose also that each player uses Optimistic Hedge to update her strategy in every round, as a function of the history of play so far. Then each player experiences O_ ( _m ·_ log _n ·_ log[4] _T_ ) _regret._
comments.explanation: The intended statement is clear from the surrounding context and from the later formal Theorem 3.1: Optimistic Hedge must be run with an appropriate learning rate/step size, depending at least on the horizon and the number of players. The abbreviated theorem is otherwise a useful high-level summary. However, as written, it sounds like the polylogarithmic regret bound holds for Optimistic Hedge with no parameter qualification. That cannot be right in general: multiplicative-weights/Optimistic Hedge guarantees are sensitive to the learning rate, and an arbitrary large or badly chosen fixed step size need not satisfy the stated regret bound. Since this is stated as a theorem rather than merely as informal prose, it would be safer to say “with an appropriate step size” or “with the step size specified in Theorem 3.1.”

comments.title: “Settle” may overstate what is shown about optimal regret
comments.quote: Indeed, despite extensive work on no-regret learning, understanding the optimal regret that can be guaranteed by no-regret learning algorithms in general-sum games has remained elusive. This question is especially intruiging in light of experiments suggesting that polylogarithmic regret should be attainable [SALS15, HAM21]. In this paper we settle this question by showing that no-regret learners can guarantee polylogarithmic regret to each player in general-sum multi-player games.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intended point: prior work left open whether the experimentally suggested polylogarithmic rates were actually attainable in general-sum multi-player games, and the paper answers that existence question positively. However, the preceding sentence frames the open problem as understanding the “optimal regret,” and the next sentence says the paper “settle[s] this question.” A polylogarithmic upper bound is a major result, but by itself it does not identify the optimal dependence on T, m, n, or the precise logarithmic powers unless matched lower bounds are also established. If the intended claim is only that polylogarithmic regret is attainable, the wording should avoid implying that the exact optimal regret rate is fully characterized.

comments.title: Lower bound for Hedge does not by itself show optimism is necessary
comments.quote: We remark that [CP20] shows that the regret of Hedge is ⌦( _pT_ ) even in 2-player games where each player has 2 actions, meaning that optimism is necessary to obtain fast rates.
comments.explanation: The cited lower bound is relevant and supports the authors’ broader motivation for studying Optimistic Hedge: plain Hedge cannot be the algorithm that achieves fast rates in this setting. But the inference stated here is stronger than what that lower bound establishes. A lower bound for Hedge rules out Hedge specifically; it does not prove that every non-optimistic algorithm fails, nor that optimism is necessary as a property of algorithms in general. There may be other algorithmic mechanisms besides optimism that achieve fast rates. The claim would be more accurate if phrased as “meaning that standard Hedge is insufficient” or “motivating the use of optimism.”

comments.title: Adversarial regret rate suppresses the standard \(\sqrt{\log n}\) dependence
comments.quote: A common goal in the literature on learning in games is to obtain an algorithm that achieves fast rates whan played by all players, and so that each player _i_ still obtains the optimal rate of _O_ ( _pT_ ) in the adversarial setting
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a high-level statement emphasizing the dependence on the horizon, and the surrounding context partly resolves it: the next sentence points to Corollary D.1 and Table 1, where the adversarial guarantee for Optimistic Hedge is stated with the usual hidden or explicit dependence on \(\log n\). Still, as written, calling \(O(\sqrt{T})\) the “optimal rate” is slightly imprecise for the finite-action full-information experts setting used here. The minimax adversarial regret is \(\Theta(\sqrt{T\log n})\) up to constants, not simply \(O(\sqrt{T})\), unless one is treating \(n\) as fixed or suppressing dimension dependence. Since the paper elsewhere tracks \(\log n\) carefully, this sentence could mislead a reader into thinking the adaptive Optimistic Hedge variant has dimension-free adversarial regret.

comments.title: Finite-difference cutoff \(H=\log T\) is not necessarily an integer
comments.quote: Let _H_ = log _T_ , where _T_ denotes the fixed time horizon from Theorem 3.1 (and thus Lemma 4.2). In the proof of Lemma 4.2, we will bound the finite differences of order _h \le H_ for certain sequences.
comments.explanation: The intended proof strategy is clear: take finite differences up to logarithmic order in the horizon. However, Definition 4.1 defines order-\(h\) finite differences only for integer \(h\), and the subsequent prose refers to the base case \(h=H\) and a downward induction from \(H\). For a general time horizon \(T\in\mathbb N\), \(\log T\) need not be an integer. This is almost certainly harmless and can be fixed by taking \(H=\lfloor \log T\rfloor\) or \(H=\lceil \log T\rceil\), with no effect on the asymptotic bound, and the appendix may well make this convention precise. But in the passage itself, the induction index is formally underspecified for non-powers of two.

comments.title: Boundary and off-by-one issues in the downward finite-difference induction
comments.quote: We next show that for all 0 _ h  H_ , _T_ P _t_ =1[Var] _x_[(] _i[t]_[)] ⇣(D _h_ +1 _`i_ )[(] _[t][−]_[1)][⌘] is bounded above by _ch ·_[P] _[T] t_ =1[Var] _x_[(] _i[t]_[)] ⇣(D _h `i_ )[(] _[t][−]_[1)][⌘] + _µh_ , for some _ch <_ 1 _/_ 2 and _µh < O_ (log[5] _T_ ). This shown via _downwards induction_ on _h_ , namely first establishing the base case _h_ = _H_ by using the result of item 1 for _h_ = _H_
comments.explanation: The intended argument is clear: compare successive finite-difference energies and then propagate a high-order bound downward to obtain the desired first-difference variance bound. However, as written, there are two related indexing problems that are not resolved by the nearby context. First, by Definition 4.1, an order-\(h\) finite difference of a length-\(T\) sequence is only defined up to time \(T-h\) (or, for the later 0-indexed convention, up to the analogous shortened endpoint). Thus the sums displayed from \(t=1\) to \(T\) involving \((D_{h+1}\ell_i)^{(t-1)}\) and \((D_h\ell_i)^{(t-1)}\) are not literally defined near the end of the horizon unless the authors are implicitly padding, truncating, or otherwise extending the sequence. Second, the stated base case at \(h=H\) involves the left-hand side with \(D_{H+1}\ell_i\), but item 1 and the abbreviated Lemma 4.4 just below are stated only for finite differences of order \(h\le H\). It may be that the appendix handles this by using \(h\le H-1\), proving the upward bound through order \(H+1\), or adding boundary padding before applying the DFT. But in this overview the induction is not quite well-defined as stated, and a careful reader cannot tell which boundary convention is intended.

comments.title: The claimed exponential decay in finite-difference order is not immediate from the displayed bound
comments.quote: First, in Lemma 4.4 below, we find an upper bound on (D _h `i_ )  _1_ for all _t 2_ [ _T_ ], _h ≥_ 0, which decays exponentially in _h_ for _h  H_ .
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: with a sufficiently small Optimistic Hedge step size and \(h\le H=\log T\), the high-order finite differences should become very small, giving the high-order base case for the later downward induction. The abbreviated Lemma 4.4 then states a bound of the form \(O(m\eta)^h\cdot h^{O(h)}\). That form can indeed decay exponentially in \(h\), but only if \(m\eta\) is small enough relative to the hidden polynomial-in-\(h\) factor; equivalently, one needs a condition such as \(m\eta\,h^C<q<1\) for the relevant hidden constant \(C\), uniformly over \(h\le H\). The surrounding step-size notation is OCR-corrupted, and the detailed appendix may well provide the necessary constants. Still, in this passage the statement that the bound “decays exponentially in \(h\)” is stronger than what the displayed \(O(m\eta)^h h^{O(h)}\) expression alone transparently implies, especially at \(h\approx \log T\). This is a mild over-compression in the proof overview rather than a clear mathematical error, but it would help to state explicitly that the chosen inverse-polylogarithmic step size dominates the hidden \(h^{O(h)}\) factor.

comments.title: Coordinatewise chain-rule bound does not by itself justify the needed \(\ell_1\) bound on strategy differences
comments.quote: To complete the inductive step it remains to upper bound the quantities _k_ (D _h xi_ )[(] _[t]_[)] _k_ 1 for _i 2_ [ _m_ ] _, t 2_ [ _T − h_ ]. ... Recalling that our goal is to bound _|_ (D _h xi_ ( _j_ ))[(] _[t]_[+1)] _|_ for each _t_ , we can do so by using Lemma 4.5 with _φ_ = _φt,i,j_ and _↵_ = _O_ ( _m⌘_ )
comments.explanation: I understand the intended reduction: because, in the two-player sketch, \(\ell_1^{(t)}=A_1x_2^{(t)}\) and the entries of \(A_1\) are bounded, one can control \(\|(D_h\ell_1)^{(t)}\|_\infty\) by an \(\ell_1\)-type norm of \((D_hx_2)^{(t)}\). The subsequent plan is to use the finite-difference chain rule for softmax coordinates to bound changes in the strategy sequence. What remains unclear is the passage from bounding each coordinate \(|(D_hx_i(j))^{(t)}|\) via a scalar softmax function \(\phi_{t,i,j}\) to the stated goal of bounding \(\|(D_hx_i)^{(t)}\|_1\) by \(O(m\eta)^h h^{O(h)}\) without incurring a factor depending on \(n_i\). A coordinatewise bound alone would generally only imply an \(n_i\)-times-larger \(\ell_1\) bound, which would be incompatible with the dimension dependence claimed elsewhere, where the regret depends on \(\log n_i\), not polynomially on \(n_i\). There may be an additional argument exploiting the normalization of softmax probabilities or total-variation control of the whole distribution, but it is not stated in this overview. Since this is an overview, the omission is localized, but as written the reduction from the scalar Lemma 4.5 application to the required \(\ell_1\) estimate is not justified.
